text: The surgeon attempts to grasp the gallbladder but opts to decompress it due to difficulty in obtaining a secure hold, resulting in the immediate release of fluid from the gallbladder.
The surgeon observes a woody infundibulum of the gallbladder, indicating chronic inflammation with visible thickening and rigidity of the tissue.
The surgeon gently pushes down on the gallbladder area, following the edematous plane, with the infundibulum not visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon carefully applies small bursts of cautery to the edematous tissue near the lateral aspect of the gallbladder, gently pushing and sweeping the tissue downwards while maintaining awareness of the nearby duodenum.
The surgeon carefully advances millimeter by millimeter, using an instrument to expose the infundibulum of the gallbladder, which is visibly woody and edematous.
The surgeon encounters pericholecystic fluid and infundibular edema while gently navigating the area, maintaining a high position on the gallbladder.
The cystic duct becomes visible as the surgeon navigates the area, noting the presence of a posterior artery nearby.
The surgeon continues to work on the posterior plane, attempting to manipulate the tissue that is curling up without yet entering the gallbladder.
The surgeon identifies the common bile duct and cystic ducts, noting the generous length of the cystic duct suitable for clip placement, ensuring a safe distance from the common bile duct.
The surgeon encounters a rigid, woody infundibulum of the gallbladder, which resists manipulation and does not conform to surrounding structures, making it challenging to push tissue away.
The surgeon continues to toggle the robotic instruments back and forth, attempting to establish the view of the cystic artery, which is not yet visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon establishes the critical view of safety by administering additional ICG dye, which causes the cystic artery to illuminate clearly, confirming its identification.
The surgeon has established the triangle of Calot and the critical view of safety, preparing to clip the cystic duct. The duct is cut first, ensuring secure placement of the clips.

The surgeon carefully removes the gallbladder from the edematous cystic plate, avoiding entry into the inflamed tissue due to acute cholecystitis. A visible one-centimeter rim of pericholecystic fluid surrounds the area.
